html:
XII

She Was a True Woman

Melbourne society was greatly agitated over the hansom cab murder. Before the assassin had been discovered it had been looked upon merely as a common murder, and one of which society need take no cognisance beyond the bare fact of its committal. But now that one of the most fashionable young men in Melbourne had been arrested as the assassin, it bade fair to assume gigantic proportions. Mrs.¬†Grundy was shocked, and openly talked about having nourished in her bosom a viper which had unexpectedly turned and stung her.

Morn, noon, and night, in Toorak drawing-rooms and Melbourne Clubs, the case formed the principal subject of conversation. And Mrs.¬†Grundy was horrified. Here was a young man, well bornвБ†вАФвАЬthe Fitzgeralds, my dear, an Irish family, with royal blood in their veinsвАЭвБ†вАФwell-bredвБ†вАФвАЬmost charming manners, I assure you, and so very good-lookingвАЭ and engaged to one of the richest girls in MelbourneвБ†вАФвАЬpretty enough, madam, no doubt, but he wanted her money, sly dog;вАЭ and this young man, who had been petted by the ladies, voted a good fellow by the men, and was universally popular, both in drawing-room and club, had committed a vulgar murderвБ†вАФit was truly shocking. What was the world coming to, and what were gaols and lunatic asylums built for if men of young FitzgeraldвАЩs calibre were not put in them, and kept from killing people? And then, of course, everybody asked everybody else who Whyte was, and why he had never been heard of before. All people who had met Mr.¬†Whyte were worried to death with questions about him, and underwent a species of social martyrdom as to who he was, what he was like, why he was killed, and all the rest of the insane questions which some people will ask. It was talked about everywhereвБ†вАФin fashionable drawing-rooms at five oвАЩclock tea, over thin bread and butter and souchong; at clubs, over brandies and sodas and cigarettes; by working men over their midday pint, and by their wives in the congenial atmosphere of the back yard over the washtub. The papers were full of paragraphs about the famous murder, and the society papers gave an interview with the prisoner by their special reporters, which had been composed by those gentlemen out of the floating rumours which they heard around, and their own fertile imaginations.

As to the prisonerвАЩs guilt, everyone was certain of it. The cabman Royston had sworn that Fitzgerald had got into the cab with Whyte, and when he got out Whyte was dead. There could be no stronger proof than that, and the general opinion was that the prisoner would put in no defence, but would throw himself on the mercy of the court. Even the church caught the contagion, and ministersвБ†вАФAnglican, Roman Catholic, and Presbyterian, together with the lesser lights of minor denominationsвБ†вАФtook the hansom cab murder as a text whereon to preach sermons on the profligacy of the age, and to point out that the only ark which could save men from the rising flood of infidelity and immorality was their own particular church. вАЬGad,вАЭ as Calton remarked, after hearing five or six ministers each claim their own church as the one special vessel of safety, вАЬthere seems to be a whole fleet of arks!вАЭ

For Mr.¬†Felix Rolleston, acquainted as he was with all concerned, the time was one of great and exceeding joy. He was ever to the fore in retailing to his friends, plus certain garnishments of his own, any fresh evidence that chanced to come to light. His endeavour was to render it the more piquant, if not dramatic. If you asked him for his definite opinion as to the innocence or guilt of the accused, Mr.¬†Felix shook his head sagaciously, and gave you to understand that neither he, nor his dear friend CaltonвБ†вАФhe knew Calton to nod toвБ†вАФhad yet been able to make up their minds about the matter.

вАЬFact is, donвАЩt you know,вАЭ observed Mr.¬†Rolleston, wisely, вАЬthereвАЩs more in this than meets the eye, and all that sort of thingвБ†вАФthink вАЩtective fellers wrong myselfвБ†вАФdonвАЩt think Fitz killed Whyte; jolly well sure he didnвАЩt.вАЭ

This would be followed invariably by a query in chorus of вАЬwho killed him then?вАЭ

вАЬAha,вАЭ Felix would retort, putting his head on one side, like a meditative sparrow; вАЬвАКвАЩtective fellers canвАЩt find out; thatвАЩs the difficulty. Good mind to go on the prowl myself, by Jove.вАЭ

вАЬBut do you know anything of the detective business?вАЭ someone would ask.

вАЬOh, dear yes,вАЭ with an airy wave of his hand; вАЬIвАЩve read Gaboreau, you know; awfully jolly life, вАЩtectives.вАЭ

Despite this evasion, Rolleston, in his heart of hearts, believed Fitzgerald guilty. But he was one of those persons, who having either tender hearts or obstinate naturesвБ†вАФthe latter is perhaps the more generalвБ†вАФdeem it incumbent upon them to come forward in championship of those in trouble. There are, doubtless, those who think that Nero was a pleasant young man, whose cruelties were but the resultant of an overflow of high spirits; and who regard Henry VIII in the light of a henpecked husband unfortunate in the possession of six wives. These people delight in expressing their sympathy with great scoundrels of the Ned Kelly order. They view them as the embodiment of heroism, unsympathetically and disgracefully treated by the narrow understanding of the law. If one half the world does kick a man when he is down, the other half invariably consoles the prostrate individual with halfpence.

And therefore, even while the weight of public opinion was dead against Fitzgerald he had his share of avowed sympathy. There was a comfort in this for Madge. Not that if the whole countryside had unanimously condemned her lover she would have believed him guilty. The element of logic does not enter into the championship of woman. Her love for a man is sufficient to exalt him to the rank of a demigod. She absolutely refuses to see the clay feet of her idol. When all others forsake she clings to him, when all others frown she smiles on him, and when he dies she reveres his memory as that of a saint and a martyr. Young men of the present day are prone to disparage their womenkind; but a poor thing is the man, who in time of trouble has no woman to stand by him with cheering words and loving comfort. And so Madge Frettlby, true woman that she was, had nailed her colours to the mast. She refused surrender to anyone, or before any argument. He was innocent, and his innocence would be proved, for she had an intuitive feeling that he would be saved at the eleventh hour. How, she knew not; but she was certain that it would be so. She would have gone to see Brian in prison, but that her father absolutely forbade her doing so. Therefore she was dependent upon Calton for all the news respecting him, and any message which she wished conveyed.

BrianвАЩs persistent refusal to set up the defence of an alibi, annoyed Calton, the more so as he could conceive no reason sufficiently worthy of the risk to which it subjected his client.

вАЬIf itвАЩs for the sake of a woman,вАЭ he said to Brian, вАЬI donвАЩt care who she is, itвАЩs absurdly quixotic. Self-preservation is the first law of nature, and if my neck was in danger IвАЩd spare neither man, woman, nor child to save it.вАЭ

вАЬI dare say,вАЭ answered Brian; вАЬbut if you had my reasons you might think differently.вАЭ

Yet in his own mind the lawyer had a suspicion which he thought might perhaps account for BrianвАЩs obstinate concealment of his movements on the fatal night. He had admitted an appointment with a woman. He was a handsome young fellow, and probably his morals were no better than those of his fellows. There was perhaps some intrigue with a married woman. He had perchance been with her on that night, and it was to shield her that he refused to speak.

вАЬEven so,вАЭ argued Calton, вАЬlet him lose his character rather than his life; indeed the woman herself should speak. It would be hard upon her I admit; yet when a manвАЩs life is in danger, surely nothing should stop her.вАЭ

Full of these perplexing thoughts, Calton went down to St.¬†Kilda to have a talk with Madge. He intended to ask her to assist him towards obtaining the information he needed. He had a great respect for Madge, and thought her a really clever woman. It was just possible, he argued, that BrianвАЩs great love might cause him to confess everything to her, at her urgent request. He found Madge awaiting his arrival with anxiety.

вАЬWhere have you been all this time?вАЭ she said as they sat down; вАЬI have been counting every moment since I saw you last. How is he?вАЭ

вАЬJust the same,вАЭ answered Calton, taking off his gloves, вАЬstill obstinately refusing to save his own life. WhereвАЩs your father?вАЭ he asked, suddenly.

вАЬOut of town,вАЭ she answered, impatiently. вАЬHe will not be back for a weekвБ†вАФbut what do you mean that he wonвАЩt save his own life?вАЭ

Calton leaned forward, and took her hand.

вАЬDo you want to save his life?вАЭ he asked.

вАЬSave his life,вАЭ she reiterated, starting up out of her chair with a cry. вАЬGod knows, I would die to save him.вАЭ

вАЬPish,вАЭ murmured Calton to himself, as he looked at her glowing face and outstretched hands, вАЬthese women are always in extremes. The fact is,вАЭ he said aloud, вАЬFitzgerald is able to prove an alibi, and he refuses to do so.вАЭ

вАЬBut why?вАЭ

Calton shrugged his shoulders.

вАЬThat is best known to himselfвБ†вАФsome quixotic idea of honour, I fancy. Now, he refuses to tell me where he was on that night; perhaps he wonвАЩt refuse to tell youвБ†вАФso you must come up and see him with me, and perhaps he will recover his senses, and confess.вАЭ

вАЬBut my father,вАЭ she faltered.

вАЬDid you not say he was out of town?вАЭ asked Calton.

вАЬYes,вАЭ hesitated Madge. вАЬBut he told me not to go.вАЭ

вАЬIn that case,вАЭ said Calton, rising and taking up his hat and gloves, вАЬI wonвАЩt ask you.вАЭ

She laid her hand on his arm.

вАЬStop! will it do any good?вАЭ

Calton hesitated a moment, for he thought that if the reason of BrianвАЩs silence was, as he surmised, an intrigue with a married woman, he might not tell the girl he was engaged to about itвБ†вАФbut, on the other hand, there might be some other reason, and Calton trusted to Madge to find it out. With these thoughts in his mind he turned round.

вАЬYes,вАЭ he answered, boldly, вАЬit may save his life.вАЭ

вАЬThen I shall go,вАЭ she answered, recklessly. вАЬHe is more to me than my father, and if I can save him, I will. Wait,вАЭ and she ran out of the room.

вАЬAn uncommonly plucky girl,вАЭ murmured the lawyer, as he looked out of the window. вАЬIf Fitzgerald is not a fool he will certainly tell her allвБ†вАФthat is, of course, if he is able toвБ†вАФqueer things these women areвБ†вАФI quite agree with BalzacвАЩs saying that no wonder man couldnвАЩt understand woman, seeing that God who created her failed to do so.вАЭ

Madge came back dressed to go out, with a heavy veil over her face.

вАЬShall I order the carriage?вАЭ she asked, pulling on her gloves with trembling fingers.

вАЬHardly,вАЭ answered Calton, dryly, вАЬunless you want to see a paragraph in the society papers to the effect that Miss Madge Frettlby visited Mr.¬†Fitzgerald in gaolвБ†вАФnoвБ†вАФnoвБ†вАФweвАЩll get a cab. Come, my dear,вАЭ and taking her arm he led her away.

They reached the station, and caught a train just as it started, yet notwithstanding this Madge was in a fever of impatience.

вАЬHow slowly it goes,вАЭ she said, fretfully.

вАЬHush, my dear,вАЭ said Calton, laying his hand on her arm. вАЬYou will betray yourselfвБ†вАФweвАЩll arrive soonвБ†вАФand save him.вАЭ

вАЬOh, God grant we may,вАЭ she said with a low cry, clasping her hands tightly together, while Calton could see the tears falling from under her thick veil.

вАЬThis is not the way to do so,вАЭ he said, almost roughly, вАЬyouвАЩll be in hysterics soonвБ†вАФcontrol yourself for his sake.вАЭ

вАЬFor his sake,вАЭ she muttered, and with a powerful effort of will, calmed herself. They soon arrived in Melbourne, and, getting a hansom, drove up quickly to the gaol. After going through the usual formula, they entered the cell where Brian was, and, when the warder who accompanied them opened the door, they found the young man seated on his bed. He looked up, and, on seeing Madge, rose and held out his hands with a cry of delight. She ran forward, and threw herself on his breast with a stifled sob. For a short time no one spokeвБ†вАФCalton being at the other end of the cell, busy with some notes which he had taken from his pocket, and the warder having retired.

вАЬMy poor darling,вАЭ said Madge, stroking back the soft, fair hair from his flushed forehead, вАЬhow ill you look.вАЭ

вАЬYes!вАЭ answered Fitzgerald, with a hard laugh. вАЬPrison does not improve a manвБ†вАФdoes it?вАЭ

вАЬDonвАЩt speak in that tone, Brian,вАЭ she said; вАЬit is not like youвБ†вАФlet us sit down and talk calmly over the matter.вАЭ

вАЬI donвАЩt see what good that will do,вАЭ he answered, wearily, as they sat down hand-in-hand. вАЬI have talked about it to Calton till my head aches, and it is no good.вАЭ

вАЬOf course not,вАЭ retorted the lawyer, sharply, as he also sat down. вАЬNor will it be any good until you come to your senses, and tell us where you were on that night.вАЭ

вАЬI tell you I cannot.вАЭ

вАЬBrian, dear,вАЭ said Madge, softly, taking his hand, вАЬyou must tell allвБ†вАФfor my sake.вАЭ

Fitzgerald sighedвБ†вАФthis was the hardest temptation he had yet been subjected to. He felt half inclined to yield, and chance the resultвБ†вАФbut one look at MadgeвАЩs pure face steeled him against doing so. What could his confession bring but sorrow and regret to one whom he loved better than his life.

вАЬMadge!вАЭ he answered, gravely, taking her hand again, вАЬyou do not know what you ask.вАЭ

вАЬYes, I do!вАЭ she replied, quickly. вАЬI ask you to save yourselfвБ†вАФto prove that you are not guilty of this terrible crime, and not to sacrifice your life for the sake ofвБ†вАФofвБ†вАФвАЭ

Here she stopped, and looked helplessly at Calton, for she had no idea of the reason of FitzgeraldвАЩs refusal to speak.

вАЬFor the sake of a woman,вАЭ finished Calton, bluntly.

вАЬA woman!вАЭ she faltered, still holding her loverвАЩs hand.

вАЬIsвБ†вАФisвБ†вАФis that the reason?вАЭ

Brian averted his face.

вАЬYes!вАЭ he said, in a low, rough voice.

A sharp expression of anguish crossed her pale face, and, sinking her head on her hands, she wept bitterly. Brian looked at her in a dogged kind of way, and Calton stared grimly at them both.

вАЬLook here,вАЭ he said, at length, to Brian, in an angry voice; вАЬif you want my opinion of your conduct I think itвАЩs infamousвБ†вАФbegging your pardon, Miss Frettlby, for the expression. Here is this noble girl, who loves you with her whole heart, and is ready to sacrifice everything for your sake, comes to implore you to save your life, and you coolly turn round and acknowledge another woman.вАЭ

Brian lifted his head haughtily, and his face flushed.

вАЬYou are wrong,вАЭ he said, turning round sharply; вАЬthere is the woman for whose sake I keep silence;вАЭ and, rising up from the bed, he pointed to Madge, as she sobbed bitterly on it. She lifted up her haggard face with an air of surprise.

вАЬFor my sake!вАЭ she cried in a startled voice.

вАЬOh, heвАЩs mad,вАЭ said Calton, shrugging his shoulders; вАЬI shall put in a defence of insanity.вАЭ

вАЬNo, I am not mad,вАЭ cried Fitzgerald, wildly, as he caught Madge in his arms. вАЬMy darling! My darling! It is for your sake that I keep silence, and I shall do so though my life pays the penalty. I could tell you where I was on that night and save myself: but if I did, you would learn a secret which would curse your life, and I dare not speakвБ†вАФI dare not.вАЭ

Madge looked up into his face with a pitiful smile as herвБ† tears fell fast.

вАЬDearest!вАЭ she said, softly. вАЬDo not think of me, but only of yourself; better that I should endure misery than that you should die. I do not know what the secret can be, but if the telling of it will save your life, do not hesitate. See,вАЭ she cried, falling on her knees, вАЬI am at your feetвБ†вАФI implore you by all the love you ever had for me, to save yourself, whatever the consequences may be to me.вАЭ

вАЬMadge,вАЭ said Fitzgerald, as he raised her in his arms, вАЬat one time I might have done so, but now it is too late. There is another and stronger reason for my silence, which I have only found out since my arrest. I know that I am closing up the one way of escape from this charge of murder, of which I am innocent; but as there is a God in heaven, I swear that I will not speak.вАЭ

There was a silence in the cell, broken only by MadgeвАЩs convulsive sobs, and even Calton, cynical man of the world as he was, felt his eyes grow wet. Brian led Madge over to him, and placed her in his arms.

вАЬTake her away,вАЭ he said, in a broken voice, вАЬor I shall forget that I am a man;вАЭ and turning away he threw himself on his bed, and covered his face with his hands. Calton did not answer him, but summoned the warder, and tried to lead Madge away. But just as they reached the door she broke away from him, and, running back, flung herself on her loverвАЩs breast.

вАЬMy darling! My darling!вАЭ she sobbed, kissing him, вАЬyou shall not die. I shall save you in spite of yourself;вАЭ and, as if afraid to trust herself longer, she ran out of the cell, followed by the barrister.
